content:
First note that any block of more than three 1’s has value ≥1111>1000, so in any solution each “block‐length’’ is 1,2, or 3.  Call by \(x,y,z\) the numbers of blocks of lengths 1,2,3 respectively.  Then

(1) \(x+2y+3z=n\),  
(2) \(1\cdot x+11\cdot y+111\cdot z=1000\),  
and \(x,y,z\ge0\) integers.

From (2) we get 
\[
x=1000-11y-111z\ge0,
\]
so \(11y\le1000-111z\), hence for each \(z=0,1,\dots,9\) we can take 
\[
y=0,1,\dots,\Big\lfloor\frac{1000-111z}{11}\Big\rfloor,
\]
and then \(x\) is determined.  The corresponding
\[
n=x+2y+3z
=(1000-111z-11y)+2y+3z
=1000-108z-9y.
\]
All these \(n\) satisfy \(n\equiv1\pmod9\).  Writing \(n=9k+1\), one finds
\[
k=111-12z-y,
\]
with \(y=0,\dots,\big\lfloor(1000-111z)/11\big\rfloor\).  A short check shows that the attainable \(k\) are exactly
\[
k=3\quad\text{and}\quad k=5,6,7,\dots,111,
\]
i.e.\ \(1\)-term \(k=3\) plus the full block \(5\) through \(111\), for a total of
\[
1+(111-5+1)=1+107=108
\]
values of \(k\), and hence \(108\) values of \(n\).  Therefore
\[
a=108,
\]
and the sum of the digits of \(a\) is
\[
1+0+8=9.
\]
\(\boxed{9}\)
